## Thromboembolism {#slide-1}

PE

DVT

## Pregnancy is a risk factor for thromboembolism {#slide-2}

Accounting for 9.3% of maternal deaths in US

Physiological changes

Mechanical obstruction by the gravid uterus

Decreased mobility

Vascular injury

## Other risk factors related to pregnancy {#slide-3}

Advanced maternal age

Obesity

Cesarean delivery

## preeclampsia {#slide-4}

Hypertension arising after 20 weeks of gestation with new onset or worsening proteinuria or end-organ disease

## Pathophysiologic association {#slide-5}

Preeclampsia 

Thromboembolism

Elevated risk for VTE in pregnancy and postpartum among those with preeclampsia.

## Retrospective cohort study {#slide-6}

The relationship between preterm preeclampsia and thromboembolic risk

## Cohorts {#slide-7}

No history of preeclampsia 

preterm preeclampsia 

Delivery at \<= 36 weeks

term preeclampsia

Delivery at \>= 37 weeks

## outcome {#slide-8}

Any thromboembolic event

Each type of thromboembolic event

## National Inpatient Sample database {#slide-9}

the largest publicly available all-payer inpatient healthcare database 

contains data from around 7 million hospital stays each year

2017-2019

2.2 million

56,446 preterm preeclampsia

86,152 term preeclampsia

## Variables of interest {#slide-10}

Demographics

Age, race, BMI, tobacco use, insurance, income, hospital bed size, hospital teaching status, length of stay, cesarean delivery

Comorbidities: grand multiparity, diabetes mellitus, chronic hypertension, asthma, anemia, history of cesarean

Outcome

PE, DVT, cerebral thrombosis or TIA, other thrombosis

## Univariate analysis {#slide-11}

Categorical variable

Continuous variable (numeric, metric)

## Slide 12

![](ppt/media/image1.png "Picture 3")

## Slide 13

![](ppt/media/image2.png "Picture 3")

## Slide 14

![](ppt/media/image3.png "Picture 3")

## Demographics {#slide-15}

Categorical 

race, income, hospital bed size, hospital teaching status

Private insurance, tobacco use, grand multiparity, diabetes mellitus, chronic hypertension, asthma, anemia, history of cesarean, cesarean delivery

Continuous 

Age, BMI, length of stay

## Slide 16

  Variables               Preterm preeclampsia n=56,446
  ----------------------- -------------------------------
  Tobacco use             3459 (6.1%)
  Chronic hypertension    8426 (14.9%)
  History of cesarean     11,434 (20.3%)

## Slide 17

  Variable                    Preterm preeclampsia n=56,446
  --------------------------- -------------------------------
  Race                        
    Non-Hispanic White        25,213 (46.2%)
    Non-Hispanic Black        13,627 (25.0%)
    Hispanic                  10,587 (19.4%)
    Asian or Pacific Island   2310 (4.2%)
    American Indian           580 (1.0%)
    Other                     2301 (4.2%)

## Video {#slide-18}

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K9teElePNkk

Descriptive statistics: 4:19-10:21

## Mean vs median {#slide-19}

Both measure central tendency

Median: reduce noise

## Slide 20

## Slide 21

  Variables   Preterm preeclampsia n=56,446
  ----------- -------------------------------
  Age         30.2 (23.8, 34.6)

## Normality test {#slide-22}

Shapiro Wilk test

swilk  age

P\<0.05

Normal distribution is not assumed

Median(IQR)

P≥0.05

Normal distribution is assumed

Mean(SD)

## Slide 23

![](ppt/media/image5.png "Picture 3")

## Slide 24

![A graph with numbers and a bar

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image6.png "Content Placeholder 3")

## Slide 25

![A graph with numbers and a bar

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image7.png "Content Placeholder 3")

## Slide 26

  Variables        Normal distribution   
  ---------------- --------------------- ------------
  Age              Yes                   Mean( sd )
  BMI              Yes                   Mean( sd )
  Length of stay   Yes                   Mean( sd )

## Slide 27

  Variables        Normal distribution   
  ---------------- --------------------- -------------
  Age              Yes                   Mean( sd )
  BMI              Yes                   Mean( sd )
  Length of stay   No                    Median(IQR)

## Slide 28

  Variables        Normal distribution   
  ---------------- --------------------- -------------
  Age              Yes                   Median(IQR)
  BMI              Yes                   Median(IQR)
  Length of stay   No                    Median(IQR)

## Slide 29

  Variables   Preterm preeclampsia n=56,446
  ----------- -------------------------------
  BMI         32.2 (23.8, 38.6)

## Slide 30

  Variables           Preterm preeclampsia n=56,446
  ------------------- -------------------------------
  BMI                 
     Under weight     923 (1.6%)
     Normal           33,158 (58.7%)
     Obesity          14,923 (26.4%)
     Morbid obesity   7442 (13.2%)

## Slide 31

  Variables           Preterm preeclampsia n=56,446
  ------------------- -------------------------------
     Obesity          14,923 (26.4%)
     Morbid obesity   7442 (13.2%)

## Slide 32

  Variables           No preeclampsia n=2,093,579   Preterm preeclampsia n=56,446
  ------------------- ----------------------------- -------------------------------
  BMI                                               
     Under weight     25,123 (1.2%)                 923 (1.6%)
     Normal           1,760,101 (84.1%)             33,158 (58.7%)
     Obesity          222,153 (10.6%)               14,923 (26.4%)
     Morbid obesity   86,202 (4.1%)                 7442 (13.2%)

## Chi-square test {#slide-33}

P\<0.05

Those with no history of preeclampsia were more likely to have normal weight.

Those with preterm preeclampsia were more likely to have obesity or morbid obesity.

## Chi-square test {#slide-34}

What if sample size is small

N=125

## Slide 35

  Variables           No preeclampsia n=79   Preterm preeclampsia n=46
  ------------------- ---------------------- ---------------------------
  BMI                                        
     Under weight     2 (2.5%)               1 (2.2%)
     Normal           66 (83.5%)             27 (58.7%)
     Obesity          8 (10.1%)              12 (26.1%)
     Morbid obesity   3 (3.8%)               6 (13.0%)

37.5% cells have a count less than 5

## Chi-square test VS Fisher's exact test {#slide-36}

\<20% 

Chi-square test or Fisher's exact test

\>= 20% and \<= 50%

Fisher's exact test

\>50%

Neither

## Slide 37

  Variables           No preeclampsia n=79   Preterm preeclampsia n=46
  ------------------- ---------------------- ---------------------------
  BMI                                        
     Under weight     2 (2.5%)               1 (2.2%)
     Normal           66 (83.5%)             27 (58.7%)
     Obesity          8 (10.1%)              12 (26.1%)
     Morbid obesity   3 (3.8%)               6 (13.0%)

37.5% cells have a count less than 5

Fisher's exact test

## Slide 38

  Observed                    No preeclampsia n=79   Preterm preeclampsia n=46
  --------------------------- ---------------------- ---------------------------
  BMI                                                
     Normal (\<30)            68 (86.1%)             28 (60.9%)
     Obesity (\>=30, \<40)    8 (10.1%)              12 (26.1%)
     Morbid obesity (\>=40)   3 (3.8%)               6 (13.0%)

16.7% cells have a count less than 5

## Slide 39

  Observed                    No preeclampsia   Preterm preeclampsia   
  --------------------------- ----------------- ---------------------- -------
  BMI                                                                  total
     Normal (\<30)            68 (86.1%)        28 (60.9%)             96
     Obesity (\>=30, \<40)    8 (10.1%)         12 (26.1%)             20
     Morbid obesity (\>=40)   3 (3.8%)          6 (13.0%)              9
  total                       79                46                     

## Slide 40

Expected Frequency=(Row Total/Grand Total)×(Column Total/Grand Total)×Grand Total=(Row  Total×Column  Total)/Grand Total

## Slide 41

  Expected                    No preeclampsia   Preterm preeclampsia   
  --------------------------- ----------------- ---------------------- -------
  BMI                                                                  total
     Normal (\<30)            60.672 (76.8%)    35.328 (76.8%)         96
     Obesity (\>=30, \<40)    12.64 (16.0%)     7.36 (16.0%)           20
     Morbid obesity (\>=40)   5.688 (7.2%)      3.312 (7.2%)           9
  total                       79                46                     

## Slide 42

  Observed             No preeclampsia   Preterm preeclampsia
  -------------------- ----------------- ----------------------
  BMI                                    
     Normal            68 (86.1%)        28 (60.9%)
     Obesity           8 (10.1%)         12 (26.1%)
     Morbid obesity    3 (3.8%)          6 (13.0%)

  Expected             No preeclampsia   Preterm preeclampsia
  -------------------- ----------------- ----------------------
  BMI                                    
     Normal            61 (76.8%)        36 (76.8%)
     Obesity           13 (16.0%)        7 (16.0%)
     Morbid obesity    6 (7.2%)          3 (7.2%)

If BMI and Preeclampsia are independent:

## Slide 43

  Variables                              No preeclampsia n=79   Preterm preeclampsia n=46
  -------------------------------------- ---------------------- ---------------------------
  BMI                                                           
     Normal (\<30)                       68 (86.1%)             28 (60.9%)
     Obesity or morbid obesity (\>=30)   11 (13.9%)             18 (39.1%)

## Slide 44

![A screenshot of a computer screen

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image8.png "Content Placeholder 4")

![A screenshot of a computer screen

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image8.png "Content Placeholder 4")

## Slide 45

![A screenshot of a computer screen

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image9.png "Content Placeholder 4")

![A screenshot of a computer screen](ppt/media/image9.png "Content Placeholder 4")

Controlled for age, race, obesity, tobacco use, chronic hypertension, asthma, anemia, chronic kidney disease, history of cesarean, multiple gestations, patient location, income, insurance, hospital location, hospital type and ownership, hospital size, length of stay, and mode of delivery

## Oral contraceptive and thromboembolism {#slide-46}

Case: with thromboembolism

Control: without thromboembolism

Exposure: oral contraceptive

## Scenario 1 {#slide-47}

Case: n=200, 15% use oral contraceptive

Control: n=200, 10% use oral contraceptive

Significance level =0.05

Power calculation

## Slide 48

![A screenshot of a computer

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image10.png "Content Placeholder 4")

## Scenario 2 {#slide-49}

Case: 15% use oral contraceptive

Control: 10% use oral contraceptive

Significance level =0.05

80% Power 

Sample size

## Slide 50

![A screenshot of a computer

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image11.png "Content Placeholder 4")

## Postpartum depression and future pregnancy {#slide-51}

Cohort 1: postpartum depression

Cohort 2: no postpartum depression

## Scenario  {#slide-52}

PPD Cohort : 40%

Non-PPD Cohort: 60%

Significance level =0.05

80% Power 

## Slide 53

![A screenshot of a computer

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image12.png "Content Placeholder 4")

## Ask UM-GPT {#slide-54}

I want to do a power/sample size calculation in Stata (R):

......

......

Can you show me how to do it step by step?

## Sampling  and weighting {#slide-55}

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DD5npelwh80

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dt0fQDabb4Y

## Hypothetical Nationwide Cesarean Rate Survey - U.S. 2024 {#slide-56}

## Hypothetical  {#slide-57}

Sample size: 4000 deliveries

35-40 hospitals or birth centers

March 1-10, 2024

## Stratified multistage cluster sampling {#slide-58}

Strata: Geographic regions, urban vs. rural areas, facility types. Clusters: Hospitals and birthing centers. 

Stage 1: Selection of Primary Sampling Units (PSUs) Stratify by geographic regions. Select PSUs using probability proportional to size (PPS) sampling based on the number of births. 

Stage 2: Within PSU Facilities Randomly select hospitals/birthing centers within each PSU. 

Stage 3: Individual Births Random sample of births from selected facilities.

## Stratified Multistage Cluster Sampling {#slide-59}

Stratified Sampling:  geographic regions, urban versus rural areas, and facility types

Cluster Sampling:

## Slide 60

![](ppt/media/image13.png "Picture 3")

## Slide 61

![](ppt/media/image13.png "Picture 3")

## weighting {#slide-62}

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SJRVxvdIc8s&t=613s

## Slide 63

![A diagram of a group of people

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image14.png "Content Placeholder 3")
